title: Ordering dim sum with family
description: Sunday yum cha — order your favourites from the trolley and check the bill
context: Yum cha (飲茶) on Sunday morning is a Hong Kong institution. Whole families gather — three generations at one table is normal. You order from a checklist sheet or flag down the trolley lady and shout what you want.
culturalFact: The phrase 'yum cha' literally means 'drink tea' — tea is the centrepiece, food is the side dish. Tapping two fingers on the table is the silent way to thank someone for refilling your cup, said to originate from a Qing dynasty Emperor travelling incognito.

lines.speaker: you
lines.jyutping: m4 goi1, ni1 dou6 dak1 gei2 do1 wai2?
lines.english: Excuse me, how many seats are available here?

lines.speaker: you
lines.cjk: 普洱，唔該。仲要蝦餃同燒賣。
lines.jyutping: pou2 lei5, m4 goi1. zung6 jiu3 haa1 gaau2 tung4 siu1 maai2.
lines.english: Pu-erh tea please. Also har gow and siu mai.

lines.speaker: you
lines.cjk: 要一籠叉燒包，同埋腸粉。
lines.jyutping: jiu3 jat1 lung4 caa1 siu1 baau1, tung4 maai4 coeng4 fan2.
lines.english: One basket of char siu bao, and cheung fun.

lines.speaker: staff
lines.jyutping: ming4 baak6, dang2 zan6 soeng5 lei4.

lines.speaker: staff
lines.jyutping: hai6 jat1 baak3 ji6 sap6 man1.
lines.english: That's one hundred and twenty dollars.
